Gene_Accession_Number.reason: Data availability lists CtF6H accession PQ040214; Results identify transcript CtAH11T0228700.1 and name CtF6H (CYP706S4).

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Paper states genes are from Carthamus tinctorius.

Applied_Species.reason: CtF6H was studied in safflower by VIGS, in Nicotiana benthamiana by transient expression, and expressed in WAT11 yeast, an engineered Saccharomyces cerevisiae strain.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: These species names are explicitly given in Results/Methods.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: CtF6H is described as a cytochrome P450 and specifically CYP706S4.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: CtF6H is directly characterized as a key enzyme in HSYA biosynthesis; this is within flavonoid metabolism.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: Results show CtF6H converts naringenin to 6-hydroxynaringenin/carthamidin, an upstream hydroxylation step toward HSYA.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: Its action precedes the final CtCGT/Ct2OGD1 step, so upstream is directly supported.

Primary_Substrate.reason: Apigenin is an explicit in vitro substrate; naringenin was shown as substrate for CtF6H in enzyme assays producing carthamidin.

Primary_Product.reason: Scutellarein from apigenin and 6-hydroxynaringenin/carthamidin from naringenin are stated.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: The paper explicitly reports 6-hydroxylation of apigenin and naringenin by CtF6H.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: Carthamidin is shown to be the productive precursor en route to HSYA, making CtF6H an immediate-precursor-forming enzyme.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: HSYA is the target metabolite throughout the study.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: HSYA is described as a quinochalcone di-C-glycoside; it is within flavonoid specialized metabolites.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Results show HSYA only in flowers; transcript data emphasize budding flowers, and white/red flower comparison supports floral accumulation context.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: Introduction states HSYA is a clinical investigational new drug for acute ischemic stroke and lists multiple protective activities.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: VIGS of CtF6H reduced HSYA and CtF6H provides carthamidin, the essential productive precursor.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: Each listed point is reported in Results: identification as F6H, in vitro hydroxylation, 30.8% HSYA reduction on VIGS, and inclusion in de novo HSYA biosynthesis in N. benthamiana.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: Candidate P450 CtAH11T0228700.1 showed high expression in budding flowers; Fig. 6e shows extremely low CtF6H expression in white-flower variant.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: The gene was analyzed in safflower budding flowers and heterologously expressed in N. benthamiana and yeast microsomes.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: Fig. 5a states CtF6H is located at the endoplasmic reticulum.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: SFLC and BiFC showed interactions of CtF6H with Ct2OGD1 and CtCGT.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: All listed methods are used for CtF6H except BiFC specifically shown for Ct2OGD1-CtCGT; however protein interaction assays including SFLC and BiFC are part of interaction analysis and CtF6H was tested by SFLC/BiFC groups.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: These experimental contexts are described in Results and Methods.

Omics_Data.reason: RNA-seq data and BioProject PRJNA1135894 are given in Data availability.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: Paper calls CtF6H a key gene/enzyme, and does not establish it as rate-limiting.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: Results show it is required in reconstruction and high expression of key genes is critical for HSYA production.

Other_Important_Info.reason: SbF6H replacement experiment is reported; low CtF6H expression in white flower is explicitly linked to lack of HSYA.

Gene_Accession_Number.reason: Data availability gives PQ040212; Results identify transcript MSTRG.178.6 and state it was named UGT708U8.

Applied_Species.reason: CtCGT was tested in safflower, N. benthamiana, and WAT11 yeast.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: These Latin names appear in the paper.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: Results describe CtCGT as a UDP-dependent glycosyltransferase, UGT708 family/type I CGT with conserved DPF motif.

Enzyme_Name.reason: Paper explicitly names CtCGT as a flavonoid di-C-glycosyltransferase.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: CtCGT is one of the key HSYA biosynthetic genes.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: Results show CtCGT performs C-glycosylation and collaborates in the last HSYA-forming step.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: CtCGT acts in the final HSYA-forming module with Ct2OGD1, although it also has standalone activity on model substrates.

Primary_Substrate.reason: Phloretin and 2-hydroxynaringenin are direct in vitro substrates; carthamidin/isocarthamidin support HSYA formation in coupled systems with Ct2OGD1.

Primary_Product.reason: Products nothofagin, 3′,5′-di-C-glycosylphloretin, 2-hydroxynaringenin mono-C-glycoside, 6,8-di-C-glycosylated 2-hydroxynaringenin, and HSYA in coordination with Ct2OGD1 are all reported.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: This summarizes the explicit enzyme assays and coupled reaction findings.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: CtCGT with Ct2OGD1 directly generates HSYA in vitro and in heterologous systems.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: HSYA is called a quinochalcone di-C-glycoside.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: HSYA is only in flowers; expression analyses emphasize budding flowers.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: HSYA activities and investigational drug status are described in Introduction.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: VIGS of CtCGT decreased HSYA content, supporting requirement for HSYA accumulation.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All five listed points are explicitly supported by Results, including 29.6% reduction, physical interaction with Ct2OGD1, and CtCHI1-enhanced yield.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: CtCGT candidate shows high expression in budding flowers.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: It was profiled in safflower and heterologously expressed in N. benthamiana and yeast.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: Fig. 5a localizes CtCGT to cytoplasm and nucleus.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: CtCGT interacts with Ct2OGD1 by SFLC/BiFC/MST and with CtF6H by SFLC/BiFC.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: All listed methods are reported for CtCGT.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: These contexts are all in Results/Methods.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: CtCGT is a key enzyme but no rate-limiting claim is made.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Discussion/Results propose F2H as a competing pathway diverting flux to common flavone C-glycosides.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: Supported by reconstruction experiments and pathway interpretation including need to avoid F2H.

Other_Important_Info.reason: UGT708U8 naming and optimum pH 9.0, 45 °C, metal-ion independence are explicitly reported.

Gene_Accession_Number.reason: Data availability gives PQ040215; Results identify CtAH01T0057600.1 as Ct2OGD1.

Source_Species.reason: Ct2OGD1 was discovered from safflower transcriptome.

Applied_Species.reason: Ct2OGD1 was tested in safflower, N. benthamiana, and yeast.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: Ct2OGD1 is annotated as a 2-oxoglutarate-dependent dioxygenase family enzyme.

Enzyme_Name.reason: The paper names it Ct2OGD1, a 2-oxoglutarate-dependent dioxygenase 1.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: It is directly validated as a key gene in HSYA biosynthesis.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: Results indicate Ct2OGD1 works with CtCGT in the final dearomatization/HSYA-forming step.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: Ct2OGD1 is required for the terminal conversion to HSYA in the proposed pathway.

Primary_Substrate.reason: Naringenin is a direct substrate in standalone assay; carthamidin and isocarthamidin are productive substrates in coupled systems with CtCGT/CtCHI1, consistent with HSYA-related intermediacy.

Primary_Product.reason: 3-hydroxynaringenin/dihydrokaempferol is produced in standalone assay; HSYA is produced in coordination with CtCGT.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: This matches the reported standalone hydroxylation and coupled last-step role.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: Ct2OGD1 directly participates in HSYA formation together with CtCGT.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: HSYA is described as a quinochalcone di-C-glycoside.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: HSYA accumulates in flowers; gene screening used budding flowers.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: HSYA biological/clinical relevance is stated in Introduction.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: VIGS of Ct2OGD1 lowered HSYA content by 26.6%.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All points are reported: co-expression identification, 26.6% reduction on VIGS, requirement in N. benthamiana with CtCGT/CtF6H, prior CtF3H annotation and standalone dihydrokaempferol production, and Fe2+/α-KG dependence.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: Ct2OGD1 was selected among high-expression budding-flower transcripts and is presented as co-expressed with key genes.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: It was profiled in safflower and heterologously expressed in N. benthamiana and yeast.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: Fig. 5a shows cytoplasm and nucleus localization.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: Ct2OGD1 interacts with CtCGT directly and with CtF6H in cellular interaction assays.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: All listed methods are explicitly used.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: Dependence on Fe2+, α-KG, and enhancement by ascorbic acid are explicitly shown; other contexts are also described.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: The study identifies Ct2OGD1 as key, not rate-limiting.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Standalone activity toward naringenin to make dihydrokaempferol is shown, but the paper does not explicitly discuss this as a metabolic tradeoff or competing flux in vivo.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: Reconstitution experiments support its essential engineering value and need for cofactors for 2OGD activity.

Other_Important_Info.reason: The prior report as CtF3H and renaming to Ct2OGD1 are explicitly stated.

Gene_Accession_Number.reason: Data availability lists CtCHI1 accession PV014870; Results identify MSTRG.27976.1 as CtCHI1.

Source_Species.reason: CtCHI1 was identified from safflower transcriptome.

Applied_Species.reason: CtCHI1 was tested in safflower expression analyses and in Saccharomyces cerevisiae yeast and in vitro; subcellular localization was in N. benthamiana, but CtCHI1 was not clearly used in N. benthamiana HSYA production assays in Results.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Carthamus tinctorius and Saccharomyces cerevisiae are clearly applied; use in Nicotiana benthamiana is for localization rather than pathway function, so broad 'applied species' inclusion is only partly supported.

Enzyme_Name.reason: CtCHI1 is explicitly called chalcone-flavanone isomerase 1.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: CtCHI1 is characterized as a key HSYA biosynthetic enzyme/yield-improving enzyme.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: Results show CtCHI1 catalyzes interconversion involving carthamidin, isocarthamidin, and likely chalcone intermediate.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: It acts before/facilitates the terminal CtCGT-Ct2OGD1 step, so a midstream-to-downstream support role is consistent with the Results.

Primary_Substrate.reason: The paper directly shows CtCHI1 converts carthamidin to isocarthamidin; although HSYA can also be made from isocarthamidin, direct CtCHI1 activity using isocarthamidin as substrate is not explicitly demonstrated.

Primary_Product.reason: Isocarthamidin from carthamidin is explicitly shown; interconversion via chalcone is proposed.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: This matches the reported conversion of carthamidin to isocarthamidin and the proposed chalcone intermediate.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: CtCHI1 improves precursor availability and boosts HSYA yield rather than directly making HSYA alone.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: HSYA is flower-specific; CtCHI1 expression pattern is analyzed in floral tissues with budding flower enrichment.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: HSYA clinical and bioactivity information is stated in Introduction.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Results support CtCHI1 as a yield-improving, not strictly indispensable, factor under tested systems.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All five points are explicitly supported by Results, including ~20-fold yeast increase and 2.4-fold in vitro increase.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: Fig. 4a shows CtCHI1 has an expression pattern similar to CtF6H, CtCGT, and Ct2OGD1, with high budding-flower expression.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: Supported for safflower and yeast; 'N. benthamiana-related assays' is vague because CtCHI1 functional HSYA assays were not reported in N. benthamiana, though localization was tested there.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: Fig. 5a localizes CtCHI1 to cytoplasm and nucleus.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: Purified enzyme assay, yeast semi-synthesis, multi-enzyme in vitro catalysis, and expression profiling are all reported.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: Budding flower stage and substrate feeding with carthamidin/isocarthamidin are described in relevant assays.

Omics_Data.reason: RNA-seq and BioProject PRJNA1135894 are provided.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: The paper presents CtCHI1 as yield-enhancing/supportive, not rate-limiting.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: Yeast and in vitro data support use of CtCHI1 to boost HSYA production with the other genes.

Other_Important_Info.reason: Supplementary Fig. 26 is explicitly cited showing CtCHI1 transcript decreases in CtF6H-, Ct2OGD1-, and CtCGT-VIGS groups.
